epigraph.text:
The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want. He makes 
me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters.
epigraph.quote: There is no royal road to geometry.

content.text: {There is no royal road to geometry.}{Euclid}

The slope-intercept form is convenient when we know the $y$-intercept, 
but what if we know a different point? The **point-slope form** 
allows us to write an equation when we know *any* point on the 
line and the slope.

Suppose a line has slope $m$ and passes through the point $(x_1, y_1)$. 
Let $(x, y)$ be any other point on the line. By the definition of slope:
\[
 m = \frac{y - y_1}{x - x_1}
\]
Multiplying both sides by $(x - x_1)$ gives us the point-slope form.

content.title: Point-Slope Form
content.content:
The **point-slope form** of a linear 
equation is:
\[
 y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)
\]
where $m$ is the slope and $(x_1, y_1)$ is a point on the line.

content.title: Writing equation given point and slope
content.problem:
Write an equation in point-slope form for the line through $(3, -2)$ 
with slope $4$.

content.title: Converting to slope-intercept form
content.problem:
Write the equation of the line through $(-1, 5)$ with slope $-2$ in 
slope-intercept form.
content.solution:
Start with point-slope form:
\[
 y - 5 = -2(x - (-1))
\]
\[
 y - 5 = -2(x + 1)
\]
Distribute and solve for $y$:
$$
 y - 5 &= -2x - 2 \\
 y &= -2x - 2 + 5 \\
 y &= -2x + 3
$$

content.text:
When given two points, we first calculate the slope, then use either 
point in the point-slope form.

content.title: Finding Equation from Two Points
content.content:
1. Calculate the slope using $m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$.

2. Choose either point as $(x_1, y_1)$.

3. Substitute into $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$.

4. Simplify to desired form.

content.title: Equation from two points
content.problem:
Write the equation of the line through $(2, 1)$ and $(5, 7)$ in 
slope-intercept form.

content.title: Equation from two points with fractions
content.problem: Write the equation of the line through $(-4, 3)$ and $(2, -1)$.

content.title: Standard Form
content.content:
The **standard form** of a linear equation is:
\[
 Ax + By = C
\]
where $A$, $B$, and $C$ are integers, and $A \geq 0$. (If $A = 0$, 
then $B > 0$.)

content.title: Converting to standard form
content.solution:
Multiply both sides by $3$ to eliminate fractions:
\[
 3y = 2x - 12
\]
Rearrange with $x$ and $y$ on the left:
\[
 -2x + 3y = -12
\]
Multiply by $-1$ so the coefficient of $x$ is positive:
\[
 2x - 3y = 12
\]

content.text: Special Cases: Horizontal and Vertical Lines

content.title: Equation of horizontal line
content.problem: Write the equation of the horizontal line through $(4, -2)$.
content.solution:
A horizontal line has slope $m = 0$. All points on the line have the 
same $y$-coordinate. The equation is:
\[
 y = -2
\]

content.title: Equation of vertical line
content.problem: Write the equation of the line through $(3, 5)$ and $(3, -1)$.
content.solution:
Both points have $x$-coordinate $3$. The slope would be:
\[
 m = \frac{-1 - 5}{3 - 3} = \frac{-6}{0} \quad \text{(undefined)}
\]
This is a **vertical line**. The equation is:
\[
 x = 3
\]

content.content:
Vertical lines cannot be written in slope-intercept or point-slope form 
because their slope is undefined. Their equation is simply $x = a$, 
where $a$ is the $x$-coordinate of every point on the line.

content.text: Summary: Three Forms of Linear Equations

marginNotes.text:
See, I am doing a new thing! Now it 
springs up; do you not perceive it?

Your word is a lamp to my feet and a 
light to my path.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
All of humanity's problems stem from man's 
inability to sit quietly in a room alone.
